message.content: Analyze the folder at C:\claude-design. Give me a comprehensive overview of: what the project is, the main apps/packages, key technologies used, the architecture, and what capabilities this system has. Be thorough but organized. List all top-level directories and their purpose, key config files, and any README/docs that explain what this is. Search breadth: very thorough.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Open Design
2	
3	Open Design is a local-first design workspace where projects contain generated design files and agent conversations. This glossary records domain language only, not implementation details.
4	
5	## Language
6	
7	**Project**:
8	A top-level design workspace that contains conversations and design files.
9	_Avoid_: repo, folder, session
10	
11	**Normal Artifact**:
12	A project design output represented by an artifact entry file and its artifact manifest.
13	_Avoid_: live artifact, generic file upload
14	
15	**Live Artifact**:
16	A refreshable project design output stored as a live-artifact record with source data and preview state.
17	_Avoid_: normal artifact, static artifact
18	
19	**Artifact Entry File**:
20	The primary project file that opens or renders a normal artifact.
21	_Avoid_: support file, asset, sidecar
22	
23	**Artifact Manifest**:
24	The sidecar metadata that identifies a project file as a normal artifact and records its kind, renderer, exports, and entry file.
25	_Avoid_: live-artifact document, project metadata
26	
27	**Active Project**:
28	The project the user most recently interacted with in the Open Design UI and that MCP tools may use when no project is specified.
29	_Avoid_: latest project, default project
30	
31	**Home Composer Media Surface**:
32	A Home-only composer intent that lets the prompt card expose media-specific defaults before project creation. The current media surfaces are `image`, `video`, `hyperframes`, and `audio`; they map onto the existing project kinds at submit time instead of extending the backend `ProjectKind` union.
33	_Avoid_: project kind, backend kind
34	
35	**Chip Rail**:
36	The row of intent chips below the Home prompt card. A chip chooses the composer surface, default scenario plugin, default option state, and project kind stamp before the user presses Run.
37	_Avoid_: plugin list, template list
38	
39	**HyperFrames Composer Surface**:
40	A standalone Home composer media surface shown between Video and Audio for HTML-based motion generation. It submits as `kind: "video"` with `videoModel: "hyperframes-html"` so persisted projects keep the existing video backend shape while the Home UI still gives HyperFrames its own entry point.
41	_Avoid_: new project kind, separate backend media kind
42	
43	**Essential Audio Generation**:
44	A Home Audio entry workflow for the audio capabilities that the product can attempt directly in v1. It includes speech and sound effects, and excludes music until there is an integrated music generation path.
45	_Avoid_: audio studio, full music workflow
46	
47	**Audio Source Field**:
48	The inline Home Audio option that provides the source content for generation. Speech uses a Text source because the content is spoken; sound effects use a Prompt source because the content describes a sound to synthesize.
49	_Avoid_: generic subject field, hidden prompt text
50	
51	**ElevenLabs Fallback Voice**:
52	The default voice option shown when the Home Audio composer cannot load configured ElevenLabs voices. It keeps ElevenLabs speech runnable by selecting the same default voice id the daemon uses when no explicit voice is supplied.
53	_Avoid_: required credential setup, empty voice selector
54	
55	## Relationships
56	
57	- A **Project** contains zero or more **Normal Artifacts**.
58	- A **Normal Artifact** has exactly one **Artifact Entry File**.
59	- A **Normal Artifact** has exactly one **Artifact Manifest**.
60	- A **Live Artifact** belongs to a **Project** but is distinct from a **Normal Artifact**.
61	- An **Active Project** can be used as the target for MCP operations when the caller omits an explicit **Project**.
62	- A **Home Composer Media Surface** maps user intent to an existing project kind and project metadata at submit time.
63	- The **Chip Rail** is the visible Home entry point for choosing a **Home Composer Media Surface**.
64	- **Essential Audio Generation** uses an **Audio Source Field** plus model options before creating an audio **Project**.
65	
66	## Example dialogue
67	
68	> **Dev:** "When a coding agent creates a Codex deck through MCP, should it create a live artifact?"
69	> **Domain expert:** "No. Unless the user asked for refreshable data, create a **Normal Artifact**: write the **Artifact Entry File** and persist its **Artifact Manifest** in the **Active Project**."
70	
71	## Flagged ambiguities
72	
73	- "artifact creation" was used to mean both **Normal Artifact** creation and **Live Artifact** creation; resolved: this capability creates **Normal Artifacts** only.

41 42 --- 43 44 ## Why this exists 45 46 Anthropic's [Claude Design][cd] (released 2026-04-17, Opus 4.7) showed what happens when an LLM stops writing prose and starts shipping design artifacts. It went viral — and stayed closed-source, paid-only, cloud-only, locked to Anthropic's model and Anthropic's skills. There is no checkout, no self-host, no Vercel deploy, no swap-in-your-own-agent. 47 48 **Open Design (OD) is the open-source alternative.** Same loop, same artifact-first mental model, none of the lock-in. We don't ship an agent — the strongest coding agents already live on your laptop. We wire them into a skill-driven design workflow that runs locally with `pnpm tools-dev`, can deploy the web layer to Vercel, and stays BYOK at every layer. 49 50 Type `make me a magazine-style pitch deck for our seed round`. The interactive question form pops up before the model improvises a single pixel. The agent picks one of five curated visual directions. A live `TodoWrite` plan streams into the UI. The daemon builds a real on-disk project folder with a seed template, layout library, and self-check checklist. The agent reads them — pre-flight enforced — runs a five-dimensional critique against its own output, and emits a single `` that renders in a sandboxed iframe seconds later. 51 52 That's not "AI tries to design something". That's an AI that has been trained, by the prompt stack, to behave like a senior designer with a working filesystem, a deterministic palette library, and a checklist culture — exactly the bar Claude Design set, but open and yours. 53 54 OD stands on four open-source shoulders: 55 56 - [**`alchaincyf/huashu-design`**](https://github.com/alchaincyf/huashu-design) — the design-philosophy compass. Junior-Designer workflow, the 5-step brand-asset protocol, the anti-AI-slop checklist, the 5-dimensional self-critique, and the "5 schools × 20 design philosophies" idea behind our direction picker — all distilled into [`apps/daemon/src/prompts/discovery.ts`](apps/daemon/src/prompts/discovery.ts). 57 - [**`op7418/guizang-ppt-skill`**](https://github.com/op7418/guizang-ppt-skill) — the deck mode. Bundled verbatim under [`skills/guizang-ppt/`](skills/guizang-ppt/) with original LICENSE preserved; magazine-style layouts, WebGL hero, P0/P1/P2 checklists. 58 - [**`OpenCoworkAI/open-codesign`**](https://github.com/OpenCoworkAI/open-codesign) — the UX north star and our closest peer. The first open-source Claude-Design alternative. Yours is good enough. 234 235 The daemon scans your `PATH` for [`claude`](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code), [`codex`](https://github.com/openai/codex), `devin`, [`cursor-agent`](https://www.cursor.com/cli), [`gemini`](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli), [`opencode`](https://opencode.ai/), [`qwen`](https://github.com/QwenLM/qwen-code), `qodercli`, [`copilot`](https://github.com/features/copilot/cli), `hermes`, `kimi`, [`pi`](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono/tree/main/packages/coding-agent), [`kiro-cli`](https://kiro.dev), `kilo`, [`vibe-acp`](https://github.com/mistralai/mistral-vibe), and `deepseek` on startup. Whichever ones it finds become candidate design engines — driven over stdio with one adapter per CLI, swappable from the model picker. Inspired by [`multica`](https://github.com/multica-ai/multica) and [`cc-switch`](https://github.com/farion1231/cc-switch). No CLI installed? The API mode is the same pipeline minus the spawn — choose Anthropic, OpenAI-compatible, Azure OpenAI, or Google Gemini and the daemon forwards normalized SSE chunks back. Loopback is allowed for local LLM providers such as Ollama and LM Studio; non-loopback private, link-local, CGNAT, multicast, reserved, and redirect targets are rejected at the daemon edge. 236 237 ### 2 · Skills are files, not plugins. 238 239 Following Claude Code's [`SKILL.md` convention](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/skills), each skill is `SKILL.md` + `assets/` + `references/`. Drop a folder into [`skills/`](skills/), restart the daemon, it appears in the picker. The bundled `magazine-web-ppt` is [`op7418/guizang-ppt-skill`](https://github.com/op7418/guizang-ppt-skill) committed verbatim — original license preserved, attribution preserved. 240 241 ### 3 · Design Systems are portable Markdown, not theme JSON. 242 243 The 9-section `DESIGN.md` schema from [`VoltAgent/awesome-design-md`][acd2] — color, typography, spacing, layout, components, motion, voice, brand, anti-patterns. Every artifact reads from the active system. Switch system → next render uses the new tokens. The dropdown ships with **Linear, Stripe, Vercel, Airbnb, Tesla, Notion, Apple, Anthropic, Cursor, Supabase, Figma, Resend, Raycast, Lovable, Cohere, Mistral, ElevenLabs, X.AI, Spotify, Webflow, Sanity, PostHog, Sentry, MongoDB, ClickHouse, Cal, Replicate, Clay, Composio, Xiaohongshu…** — plus 57 design skills sourced from [`awesome-design-skills`][ads]. 244 245 ### 4 · The interactive question form prevents 80% of redirects. 246 247 OD's prompt stack hard-codes a `RULE 1`: every fresh design brief begins with a `` instead of code. Surface · audience · tone · brand context · scale · constraints. A long brief still leaves design decisions open — visual tone, color stance, scale — exactly the things the form locks down in 30 seconds. The cost of a wrong direction is one chat round, not one finished deck. 248 249 This is the **Junior-Designer mode** distilled from [`huashu-design`](https://github.com/alchaincyf/huashu-design): batch the questions up front, show something visible early (even a wireframe with grey blocks), let the user redirect cheaply. Combined with the brand-asset protocol (locate · download · `grep` hex · write `brand-spec.md` · vocalise), it's the single biggest reason output stops feeling like AI freestyle and starts feeling like a designer who paid attention before painting. 250 251 ### 5 · The daemon makes the agent feel like it's on your laptop, because it is. 252 253 The daemon spawns the CLI with `cwd` set to the project's artifact folder under `.od/projects//`. The agent gets `Read`, `Write`, `Bash`, `WebFetch` — real tools against a real filesystem. It can `Read` the skill's `assets/template.html`, `grep` your CSS for hex values, write a `brand-spec.md`, drop generated images, and produce `.pptx` / `.zip` / `.pdf` files that show up in the file workspace as download chips when the turn ends. Sessions, conversations, messages, tabs persist in a local SQLite DB — pop the project open tomorrow and the agent's todo card is right where you left it. 254 255 ### 6 · The prompt stack is the product. 256 257 What you compose at send time isn't "system + user". It's: 258 259 ``` 260 DISCOVERY directives (turn-1 form, turn-2 brand branch, TodoWrite, 5-dim critique) 261 + identity charter (OFFICIAL_DESIGNER_PROMPT, anti-AI-slop, junior-pass) 262 + active DESIGN.md (150 systems available) 263 + active SKILL.md (132 skills available) 264 + project metadata (kind, fidelity, speakerNotes, animations, inspiration ids) 265 + skill side files (auto-injected pre-flight: read assets/template.html + references/*.md) 266 + (deck kind, no skill seed) DECK_FRAMEWORK_DIRECTIVE (nav / counter / scroll / print) 267 ``` 268 269 Every layer is composable. Every layer is a file you can edit. Read [`apps/daemon/src/prompts/system.ts`](apps/daemon/src/prompts/system.ts) and [`apps/daemon/src/prompts/discovery.ts`](apps/daemon/src/prompts/discovery.ts) to see the actual contract. 270 271 ## Architecture 272 273 ``` 274 ┌────────────────────── browser (Next.js 16) ──────────────────────┐ 275 │ chat · file workspace · iframe preview · settings · imports │ 276 └──────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┬───────────────┘ 277 │ /api/* (rewritten in dev) │ 278 ▼ ▼ 279 ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ /api/proxy/{provider}/stream (SSE) 280 │ Local daemon (Express + SQLite) │ ─→ any OpenAI-compat 281 │ │ endpoint (BYOK) 282 │ /api/agents /api/skills│ w/ SSRF blocking 283 │ /api/design-systems /api/projects/… 284 │ /api/chat (SSE) /api/proxy/{provider}/stream (SSE) 285 │ /api/templates /api/import/claude-design 286 │ /api/artifacts/save /api/artifacts/lint 287 │ /api/upload /api/projects/:id/files… 288 │ /artifacts (static) /frames (static) 289 │ 290 │ optional: sidecar IPC at /tmp/open-design/ipc//.sock 291 │ (STATUS · EVAL · SCREENSHOT · CONSOLE · CLICK · SHUTDOWN) 292 └─────────┬────────────────────────┘ 293 │ spawn(cli, [...], { cwd: .od/projects/ }) 294 ▼ 295 ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ 296 │ claude · codex · devin (ACP) · gemini · opencode · cursor-agent │ 297 │ qwen · qoder · copilot · hermes (ACP) · kimi (ACP) · pi (RPC) · kiro (ACP) · kilo (ACP) · vibe (ACP) · deepseek │ 298 │ reads SKILL.md + DESIGN.md, writes artifacts to disk │ 299 └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ 300 ``` 301 302 | Layer | Stack | 303 |---|---| 304 | Frontend | Next.js 16 App Router + React 18 + TypeScript, Vercel-deployable | 305 | Daemon | Node 24 · Express · SSE streaming · `better-sqlite3`; Click **Save to disk** or download as a project ZIP. 400 401 ### First-run state (`./.od/`) 402 403 The daemon owns one hidden folder at the repo root. Everything in it is gitignored and machine-local — never commit it. 404 405 ``` 406 .od/ 407 ├── app.sqlite ← projects · conversations · messages · open tabs 408 ├── artifacts/ ← one-off "Save to disk" renders (timestamped) 409 └── projects// ← per-project working dir, also the agent's cwd 410 ``` 411 412 | Want to… | Do this | 413 |---|---| 414 | Inspect what's in there | `ls -la .od && sqlite3 .od/app.sqlite '.tables'` | 415 | Reset to a clean slate | `pnpm tools-dev stop`, `rm -rf .od`, run `pnpm tools-dev run web` again | 416 | Move it elsewhere | `OD_DATA_DIR= pnpm tools-dev run web` — the daemon resolves `~/` and anchors relative paths to the repo root. `OD_MEDIA_CONFIG_DIR=` narrows the override to just `media-config.json` if you want credentials in a separate location. | 417 418 #### Migrating a pre-desktop-app `.od/` into the installed Desktop app 419 420 If you ran the repo first and only later installed the packaged Desktop app, the two writers point at different roots: 421 422 - Repo dev-server (`pnpm tools-dev start web`) writes to `/.od/`. 423 - Installed Desktop app writes under `/Open Design/namespaces//data/`, where `` is Electron's per-OS app-data base (everything before the `Open Design` segment that `app.getPath("userData")` already includes). The channel suffix is **platform-specific** — the release workflows append `-win`/`-linux`: 424 425 | Platform | `` (Electron `appData` base) | Stable channel | Beta channel | 426 |---|---|---|---| 427 | macOS | `~/Library/Application Support` | `release-stable` | `release-beta` | 428 | Windows | `%APPDATA%` (= `%USERPROFILE%\AppData\Roaming`) | `release-stable-win` | `release-beta-win` | 429 | Linux | `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME` (default `~/.config`) | `release-stable-linux` | `release-beta-linux` | 430 431 Example resolved paths: 432 - macOS beta: `~/Library/Application Support/Open Design/namespaces/release-beta/data/` 433 - Windows beta: `%APPDATA%\Open Design\namespaces\release-beta-win\data\` 434 - Linux beta: `~/.config/Open Design/namespaces/release-beta-linux/data/` 435 436 If unsure, inspect the packaged daemon log right after the app boots; it logs the resolved `daemonDataRoot`. 437 438 > **⚠️ Do this in a clean state.** Migration replaces (not merges) the Desktop app's data dir with your repo `.od/`. Both writers must be fully stopped before copying — quit the Desktop app **and** stop the repo dev-server. SQLite-WAL needs to flush cleanly on both sides; if either daemon is still running it can write SQLite/WAL pages or project/artifact files mid-snapshot, leaving the staged copy inconsistent. If the Desktop app already has projects you care about, decide which side is authoritative before continuing — the steps below back up the Desktop's current `data/` to a sibling but do not merge. 439 440 ##### Option A: one-shot auto-migration via `OD_LEGACY_DATA_DIR` 441 442 Use this when the Desktop app's `data/` is still empty, which is the typical state right after the upgrade that surfaced [#710](https://github.com/nexu-io/open-design/issues/710). Quit the Desktop app first (so its daemon is not holding `app.sqlite`), then re-launch with `OD_LEGACY_DATA_DIR` pointed at your old repo `.od/`. The daemon stages your payload into a sibling tmp directory and only promotes it into `data/` on success; on any failure the staging directory is removed so the next boot retries cleanly. 443 444 The daemon refuses, with a visible startup error, when: 445 446 - the path in `OD_LEGACY_DATA_DIR` does not contain `app.sqlite` (typo, deleted source, wrong path), or 447 - the Desktop's `data/` already contains any of `app.sqlite`, `projects/`, `artifacts/`, `media-config.json`, etc. SQLite/WAL pairs and project trees cannot be safely interleaved, so the daemon refuses to merge instead of silently corrupting either side. If the Desktop has already booted and seeded its own `data/`, use Option B and decide explicitly which side wins. 448 449 A `.migrated-from` marker is written on success so subsequent boots no-op. 450 451 Quit the Desktop app first, then re-launch with this env set. The launcher must put the variable into the *app process* environment, not just the shell that runs `open` / `xdg-open`. 452 453 **macOS** (LaunchServices does not inherit shell env, so use the direct binary): 454 455 ```bash 456 OD_LEGACY_DATA_DIR="/path/to/old/repo/.od" \ 457 "/Applications/Open Design.app/Contents/MacOS/Open Design" 458 ``` 459 460 If you prefer the Dock launcher, set the variable in `launchctl` first, open the app, then unset it: 461 462 ```bash 463 launchctl setenv OD_LEGACY_DATA_DIR "/path/to/old/repo/.od" 464 open "/Applications/Open Design.app" 465 # After the migration log line appears: 466 launchctl unsetenv OD_LEGACY_DATA_DIR 467 ``` 468 469 **Linux** (run the binary directly so the env var actually reaches it): 470 471 ```bash 472 OD_LEGACY_DATA_DIR="/path/to/old/repo/.od" /path/to/open-design 473 # (e.g. the AppImage you launched, or the unpacked binary under /opt) 474 ``` 475 476 **Windows (PowerShell):** 477 478 ```powershell 479 $env:OD_LEGACY_DATA_DIR="C:\path\to\old\repo\.od" 480 & "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Programs\Open Design\Open Design.exe" 481 ``` 482 483 The daemon log records `[od-migrate] migration complete: copied N entries (...)`. After the first launch you can clear the env variable; the marker prevents re-migration even on subsequent runs. 484 485 ##### Option B: manual copy 486 487 To carry your existing projects, SQLite, artifacts, and `media-config.json` over to the Desktop app, when Option A is not viable (Desktop already has its own data and you want to replace it explicitly). 488 489 **macOS / Linux (bash):** 490 491 ```bash 492 set -euo pipefail 493 # 1. Stop both writers so the source and target are quiescent. 494 # - Quit the Desktop app (Cmd+Q on macOS, File → Exit on Linux). 495 # - Stop the repo dev-server: `pnpm tools-dev stop` from the repo root. 496 # 2. Set REPO and APP_DATA to your actual paths; the example below is macOS + beta. 497 REPO="/path/to/open-design" 498 APP_DATA="$HOME/Library/Application Support/Open Design/namespaces/release-beta/data" 499 500 # 3. Preflight: see what (if anything) the Desktop app already has. 501 ls "$APP_DATA/projects" 2>/dev/null && echo "Desktop already has projects, confirm this is a replace, not a merge." 502 503 # 4. Stage into a sibling first, then atomically swap into place. `set -e` plus 504 # the explicit rsync exit check guarantee a non-zero copy aborts before any 505 # `mv` runs, so the Desktop data dir cannot end up half-populated. 506 STAGE="${APP_DATA}.staged-$(date +%F-%H%M)" 507 mkdir -p "$STAGE" 508 rsync -a --exclude='backup-*' "$REPO/.od/" "$STAGE/" || { echo "rsync failed, aborting before swap"; exit 1; } 509 510 # 5. Backup the Desktop's current data, then promote the staged copy. 511 mv "$APP_DATA" "${APP_DATA}.fresh-baseline-$(date +%F-%H%M)" 512 mv "$STAGE" "$APP_DATA" 513 514 # 6. Relaunch the Desktop app. The daemon applies forward schema changes on boot. 515 ``` 516 517 **Windows (PowerShell):** 518 519 ```powershell 520 $ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop' 521 # 1. Stop both writers so the source and target are quiescent. 522 # - Quit the Desktop app (File > Exit). 523 # - Stop the repo dev-server: `pnpm tools-dev stop` from the repo root. 524 # 2. Set $Repo and $AppData to your actual paths; the example below is stable channel. 525 $Repo = 'C:\path\to\open-design' 526 $AppData = Join-Path $env:APPDATA 'Open Design\namespaces\release-stable-win\data' 527 528 # 3. Preflight: see what (if anything) the Desktop app already has. 529 if (Test-Path (Join-Path $AppData 'projects')) { 530 Write-Host 'Desktop already has projects, confirm this is a replace, not a merge.' 531 } 532 533 # 4. Stage into a sibling first. Robocopy /MIR mirrors source to staging, and 534 # its exit codes >= 8 are real errors (0..7 are success/info), so we guard 535 # explicitly before promoting. 536 $Stamp = Get-Date -Format 'yyyy-MM-dd-HHmm' 537 $Stage = "$AppData.staged-$Stamp" 538 robocopy "$Repo\.od" $Stage /MIR /XD 'backup-*' | Out-Null 539 if ($LASTEXITCODE -ge 8) { throw "robocopy failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE), aborting before swap" } 540 541 # 5. Backup the Desktop's current data, then promote the staged copy. 542 if (Test-Path $AppData) { Rename-Item $AppData "$AppData.fresh-baseline-$Stamp" } 543 Rename-Item $Stage $AppData 544 545 # 6. Relaunch the Desktop app. The daemon applies forward schema changes on boot. 546 ``` 547 548 If anything looks wrong after relaunch, restore the original Desktop data by deleting `$APP_DATA` (or `$AppData` on Windows) and renaming the `.fresh-baseline-*` directory back into place. 549 550 > **⚠️ Schema migrations are forward-only.** The daemon applies `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` / `ALTER TABLE` changes on boot; there is no version guard. After migrating, **do not** open the same data dir with an older repo checkout — unsupported columns or behavior mismatches can leave the workspace inconsistent. Back up `app.sqlite*` before the first launch with the new app. 551 552 > **⚠️ Advanced: sharing one data dir between repo dev-server and Desktop app.** Pointing both at the same dir via `OD_DATA_DIR` is possible but **only safe one-at-a-time**. The daemon opens `app.sqlite` in WAL mode and writes uncoordinated files under `projects/` and `artifacts/`; running both writers concurrently can corrupt SQLite or clobber artifacts. Always stop the Desktop app before starting the dev-server, and stop the dev-server before opening the Desktop app: 553 > 554 > ```bash 555 > OD_DATA_DIR="$HOME/Library/Application Support/Open Design/namespaces/release-beta/data" \ 556 > pnpm tools-dev start web 557 > ``` 558 559 Full file map, scripts, and troubleshooting → [`QUICKSTART.md`](QUICKSTART.md). 560 561 ## Running the Project 562 563 Open Design can run as a web app in your browser or as an Electron desktop application. Both modes share the same local daemon + web architecture. 564 565 ### Web / Localhost (Default) 566 567 ```bash 568 # Foreground mode — keeps the lifecycle command in the foreground (logs written to files) 569 pnpm tools-dev run web 570 571 # View recent logs: 572 pnpm tools-dev logs 573 574 # Background mode — daemon + web run as background processes 575 pnpm tools-dev start web 576 ``` 577 578 By default, `tools-dev` binds to available ephemeral ports and prints the actual URLs on startup. To use fixed ports from a stopped state: 579 580 ```bash 581 pnpm tools-dev run web --daemon-port 17456 --web-port 17573 582 ``` 583 584 If daemon/web are already running, use `restart` to switch ports in the existing session: 585 586 ```bash 587 pnpm tools-dev restart --daemon-port 17456 --web-port 17573 588 ``` 589 590 ### Desktop / Electron 591 592 ```bash 593 # Start daemon + web + desktop in the background 594 pnpm tools-dev 595 596 # Check desktop status 597 pnpm tools-dev inspect desktop status 598 599 # Take a screenshot of the desktop app 600 pnpm tools-dev inspect desktop screenshot --path /tmp/open-design.png 601 ``` 602 603 The desktop app discovers the web URL automatically via sidecar IPC — no port guessing required. 604 605 ### Other Useful Commands 606 607 | Command | What it does | 608 |---|---| 609 | `pnpm tools-dev status` | Show running sidecar statuses | 610 | `pnpm tools-dev logs` | Show daemon/web/desktop log tails | 611 | `pnpm tools-dev stop` | Stop all running sidecars | 612 | `pnpm tools-dev restart` | Stop then restart all sidecars | 613 | `pnpm tools-dev check` | Status + recent logs + common diagnostics | 614 615 For fixed-port restarts, background startup, and full troubleshooting see [`QUICKSTART.md`](QUICKSTART.md). 616 617 ## Nix 618 619 A flake is published at the repo root. Home Manager is the recommended path for individual developers; a NixOS module is also exposed for shared/server installs. See [`nix/README.md`](nix/README.md) for the full surface (data dir, secrets, `webFrontend` vs. bringing your own server, `OD_DAEMON_URL`). 620 621 ```nix 622 # Home Manager 623 inputs.open-design.url = "github:nexu-io/open-design"; 624 # then: imports = [ inputs.open-design.homeManagerModules.default ]; 625 ``` 626 627 ```bash 628 nix run github:nexu-io/open-design # boot the daemon (`od`) without installing 629 ``` 630 631 For developers, a Nix dev shell is available and can be used with `direnv` too: 632 633 ```bash 634 nix develop # dev shell with required dependencies to work on Open Design 635 ``` 636 637 638 ## Use Open Design from your coding agent 639 640 Open Design ships a stdio MCP server. Wire it into Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, VS Code, Antigravity, Zed, Windsurf, or any MCP-compatible client and the agent in another repo can read files from your local Open Design projects directly. Replaces the export-then-attach loop. When the agent calls `search_files`, `get_file`, or `get_artifact` without a project argument, the MCP defaults to whatever project (and file) you have open in Open Design right now, so prompts like *"build this in my app"* or *"match these styles"* just work. 641 642 **Why MCP?** Exporting and re-attaching a zip every design iteration breaks flow. The MCP server exposes your design source directly -- tokens CSS, JSX components, entry HTML -- as a structured API the agent can query by name. The agent always sees the live file, not a stale copy from the last export. 643 644 Open **Settings → MCP server** in the Open Design app for a per-client install flow. The panel bakes the absolute path to your `node` binary and the daemon's built `cli.js` into every snippet, so it works on a fresh source clone where `od` is not on your PATH. Cursor gets a one-click deeplink; the rest get a copy-paste JSON snippet in the schema their config file expects (Claude Code includes a `claude mcp add-json` one-liner so you do not have to hand-edit `~/.claude.json`). Restart or reload your client after install for the server to show up. 645 646 The daemon must be running locally for MCP tool calls to succeed. If the agent was started before Open Design, restart the agent after Open Design is up so it can reach the live daemon. Tool calls made while the daemon is offline return a clear `"daemon not reachable"` error rather than a crash. 647 648 **Security model.** The MCP server is read-only; it exposes file reads, file metadata, and search -- nothing that writes to disk or calls an external service. It runs as a child process of the coding agent over stdio, so any MCP client you register inherits read access to your local Open Design projects. Treat it like installing a VS Code extension: only register clients you trust. The daemon binds to `127.0.0.1` by default; LAN-wide exposure requires an explicit `OD_BIND_HOST` opt-in. If you also front the SPA with a non-loopback static server, set `OD_ALLOWED_ORIGINS=,,...` (comma-separated `scheme://host[:port]` entries) so the daemon's same-origin gate accepts API writes from those origins on both the `Origin` and `Host` checks; without it the browser will see 403s on every PUT/POST (Caddy v2 reverse_proxy preserves the original Host header upstream by default, so loopback alone is not enough). Connector-credential and live-artifact preview routes stay loopback-only regardless. 649 650 ## Repository structure 651 652 ``` 653 open-design/ 654 ├── README.md ← this file 655 ├── README.de.md ← 1	# Directory guide
2	
3	This file is the single source of truth for agents entering this repository. Read this file first; after entering `apps/`, `packages/`, `tools/`, or `e2e/`, read that layer's `AGENTS.md` for module-level details. Do not copy module details back into the root file; root stays focused on cross-repository boundaries, workflow, and commands.
4	
5	## Core documentation index
6	
7	- Product and onboarding: `README.md`, `README.zh-CN.md`, `QUICKSTART.md`.
8	- Contribution and environment: `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.zh-CN.md`.
9	- Architecture and protocols: `docs/spec.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, `docs/skills-protocol.md`, `docs/agent-adapters.md`, `docs/modes.md`.
10	- Roadmap and references: `docs/roadmap.md`, `docs/references.md`, `docs/code-review-guidelines.md`, `specs/current/maintainability-roadmap.md`.
11	- Directory-level agent guidance: `apps/AGENTS.md`, `packages/AGENTS.md`, `tools/AGENTS.md`, `e2e/AGENTS.md`.
12	- Packaged auto-update architecture and high-confidence local harness: read `tools/pack/AGENTS.md` section "Packaged auto-update architecture and harness" before touching packaged updater code, release-channel identity, installer behavior, or updater UI.
13	
14	## Workspace directories
15	
16	- Workspace packages come from `pnpm-workspace.yaml`: `apps/*`, `packages/*`, `tools/*`, and `e2e`.
17	- Top-level content directories: `skills/` (functional skills the agent invokes mid-task — utilities, briefs, packagers; see `skills/AGENTS.md`), `design-templates/` (rendering catalogue: decks, prototypes, image/video/audio templates; see `design-templates/AGENTS.md` and `specs/current/skills-and-design-templates.md`), `design-systems/` (brand `DESIGN.md` files), `craft/` (universal brand-agnostic craft rules a skill can opt into via `od.craft.requires`).
18	- `apps/web` is the Next.js 16 App Router + React 18 web runtime; do not restore `apps/nextjs`.
19	- `apps/daemon` is the local privileged daemon and `od` bin. It owns `/api/*`, agent spawning, skills, design systems, artifacts, and static serving.
20	- `apps/desktop` is the Electron shell; it discovers the web URL through sidecar IPC.
21	- `apps/packaged` is the thin packaged Electron runtime entry; it starts packaged sidecars and owns the `od://` entry glue only.
22	- `packages/contracts` is the pure TypeScript web/daemon app contract layer.
23	- `packages/sidecar-proto` owns the Open Design sidecar business protocol; `packages/sidecar` owns the generic sidecar runtime; `packages/platform` owns generic OS process primitives.
24	- `tools/dev` is the local development lifecycle control plane.
25	- `tools/pack` is the local packaged build/start/stop/logs control plane, packaged updater harness, installer identity/registry validation surface, and mac beta release artifact preparation surface.
26	- `tools/pr` is the maintainer PR-duty control plane: a thin `gh` wrapper that encodes this repo's review-lane derivation, forbidden-surface flags, lane checklists, and validation-command suggestions.
27	- `tools/serve` is the local fixture-service control plane; first service is `tools-serve start updater` for deterministic updater metadata and artifacts.
28	- `e2e` owns user-level end-to-end smoke tests and Playwright UI automation; read `e2e/AGENTS.md` before editing its tests or commands.
29	
30	## Inactive or placeholder directories
31	
32	- `apps/nextjs` and `packages/shared` have been removed; do not recreate or reference them.
33	- `.od/`, `.tmp/`, Playwright reports, and agent scratch directories are local runtime data and must stay out of git.
34	
35	# Development workflow
36	
37	## Environment baseline
38	
39	- Runtime target is Node `~24` and `pnpm@10.33.2`; use Corepack so the pnpm version pinned in `package.json` is selected.
40	- New project-owned entrypoints, modules, scripts, tests, reporters, and configs should default to TypeScript.
41	- Residual JavaScript is limited to generated output, vendored dependencies, explicitly documented compatibility build artifacts, and the allowlist in `scripts/guard.ts`.
42	
43	## Windows native
44	
45	- macOS, Linux, and WSL2 are the primary supported paths. Windows native is best-effort — file an issue if it doesn't work.
46	- Historical Windows-specific friction is documented in closed issues #10, #96, #100, #203, and #315; check the issue tracker for the current state before filing new reports.
47	- Install Node 24. Either `winget install OpenJS.NodeJS.LTS` (currently Node 24.x) or download from https://nodejs.org. After install, verify with `node --version` — the WinGet LTS pointer rolls to the next major in October 2026, so re-verify if you re-run the install command later. Do not use Node 22 — see FAQ.
48	- `corepack enable` fails with EPERM on Windows (cannot write shims to `Program Files`). Use `npm install -g pnpm@10.33.2` instead.
49	- `better-sqlite3` has no prebuilt binary for win32/Node 24; `pnpm install` will compile it from source via node-gyp (~2 min). Requires Visual Studio Build Tools 2022 or newer. This is expected — not a sign of version incompatibility.
50	- For `tools-dev` start/stop/status usage, see "Local lifecycle" below.
51	
52	## Local lifecycle
53	
54	- Use `pnpm tools-dev` as the only local development lifecycle entry point.
55	- Do not add or restore root lifecycle aliases: `pnpm dev`, `pnpm dev:all`, `pnpm daemon`, `pnpm preview`, or `pnpm start`.
56	- Ports are governed by `tools-dev` flags: `--daemon-port` and `--web-port`.
57	- `tools-dev` exports `OD_PORT` for the web proxy target and `OD_WEB_PORT` for the web listener; do not use `NEXT_PORT`.
58	
59	## Root command boundary
60	
61	- Keep root scripts reserved for true repo-level checks and tools control-plane entrypoints: `pnpm guard`, `pnpm typecheck`, `pnpm tools-dev`, `pnpm tools-pack`, `pnpm tools-pr`, and `pnpm tools-serve`.
62	- Do not add root aggregate `pnpm build` or `pnpm test` aliases. Build/test commands must stay package-scoped (`pnpm --filter <package> ...`) or tool-scoped (`pnpm tools-pack ...` / `pnpm tools-pr ...`).
63	- Do not add root e2e aliases; e2e package commands and ownership rules live in `e2e/AGENTS.md`.
64	
65	## Release channel model
66	
67	- `beta` is the daily R&D/development validation channel. It is optimized for fast development feedback and is not part of the stable promotion gate.
68	- `nightly` is the internal validation channel for stable delivery. Stable releases remain gated by validated nightly artifacts.
69	- `preview` is an independent early-access channel with stable-like release rigor. It should use preview versions such as `X.Y.Z-preview.N`, publish to the `preview` R2 channel, publish updater feeds under `preview/latest`, and follow stable's platform policy including the existing optional Linux enablement.
70	- `stable` is the formal delivery channel. Do not make stable promotion depend on preview; stable continues to depend on nightly only.
71	- Public packaged app identity must stay channel-distinct: stable uses `Open Design`, beta uses `Open Design Beta`, and preview uses `Open Design Preview`. Do not ship beta or preview mac DMGs whose drag-install app bundle is `Open Design.app`.
72	- Windows beta updater validation must use the real beta namespace `release-beta-win`; otherwise a local beta-like namespace can create a separate uninstall registry key while looking like the same `Open Design Beta` app. See `tools/pack/AGENTS.md` for the architecture map and high-confidence acceptance harness.
73	
74	## Boundary constraints
75	
76	- Tests under `apps/`, `packages/`, and `tools/` live in a package/app/tool-level `tests/` directory sibling to `src/`; keep `src/` source-only and do not add new `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` files under `src/`. Playwright UI automation belongs to `e2e/ui/`, not app packages.
77	- App packages must not import another app's private `src/` or `tests/` implementation as a shared helper. In particular, `apps/web/**` must not import `apps/daemon/src/**`; web/daemon integration belongs behind HTTP APIs, `packages/contracts`, and app-local provider boundaries.
78	- Cross-app, cross-runtime, or repository-resource consistency checks belong in `e2e/tests/` when they need to observe more than one app/package boundary; promote reusable logic to a pure package instead of borrowing another app's private source.
79	- Keep shared API DTOs, SSE event unions, error shapes, task shapes, and example payloads in `packages/contracts`; update contracts before wiring divergent web/daemon request or response shapes.
80	- Keep `packages/contracts` pure TypeScript and free of Next.js, Express, Node filesystem/process APIs, browser APIs, SQLite, daemon internals, and sidecar control-plane dependencies.
81	- Keep project-owned entrypoints, modules, scripts, tests, reporters, and configs TypeScript-first; generated `dist/*.js` is runtime output, and source edits belong in `.ts` files.
82	- New `.js`, `.mjs`, or `.cjs` files need an explicit generated/vendor/compatibility reason and must pass `pnpm guard`.
83	- App business logic must not know about sidecar/control-plane concepts. Keep sidecar awareness in `apps/<app>/sidecar` or the desktop sidecar entry wrapper.
84	- Shared web/daemon app contracts belong in `packages/contracts`; that package must not depend on Next.js, Express, Node filesystem/process APIs, browser APIs, SQLite, daemon internals, or the sidecar control-plane protocol.
85	- Sidecar process stamps must have exactly five fields: `app`, `mode`, `namespace`, `ipc`, and `source`.
86	- Orchestration layers (`tools-dev`, `tools-pack`, packaged launchers) must call package primitives; do not hand-build `--od-stamp-*` args or process-scan regexes.
87	- Packaged runtime paths must be namespace-scoped and independent from daemon/web ports; ports are transient transport details only.
88	- Default runtime files live under `<project-root>/.tmp/<source>/<namespace>/...`; POSIX IPC sockets are fixed at `/tmp/open-design/ipc/<namespace>/<app>.sock`.
89	
90	## Capability exposure (UI/CLI dual-track)
91	
92	Every user-facing capability must be reachable through both the web UI **and** the `od` CLI (`apps/daemon/src/cli.ts`). Shipping a feature with only one of the two surfaces is a regression.
93	
94	- The CLI is the embeddability contract. External agents (hermes-agent, openclaw, custom Slack/Discord bots, packaged runtimes invoked from another shell) drive Open Design through `od` subcommands — they do not render the web UI. If a capability is UI-only, it cannot be composed into those external agents.
95	- Both surfaces must call the same `/api/*` endpoints; do not let the CLI talk to one shape and the UI to another. The daemon HTTP layer is the single source of truth, with `packages/contracts` carrying the shared DTOs.
96	- The CLI form must support `--json` for machine-readable output and accept long-form prompts via `--prompt-file <path|->`, so jobs that pipe through `xargs`, `jq`, and `<heredoc` stay clean.
97	- Adding a new capability is a three-step closure: HTTP endpoint in `apps/daemon/src/*-routes.ts` (with a contract type in `packages/contracts/src/api/`), UI surface in `apps/web/src/`, and `od <capability>` subcommand in `apps/daemon/src/cli.ts` registered through `SUBCOMMAND_MAP`. Land all three in the same PR; do not stage them across PRs.
98	- The PR template's Surface area checklist must reflect *both* surfaces. If you ticked UI, tick CLI too — and vice-versa — or explain in the PR body why the missing surface is genuinely not applicable (e.g. an internal-only daemon health probe). "I'll do the CLI later" is not a valid reason.
99	- Existing reference points: `od automation …` mirrors the Automations tab against `/api/routines`; `od plugin …`, `od ui …`, `od project …`, `od media …`, `od mcp …`, `od research …` follow the same shape. Copy that pattern for new capabilities.
100	
101	## Git commit policy
102	
103	- Git commits must not include `Co-authored-by` trailers or any other co-author metadata.
104	
105	## Pull request expectations
106	
107	- Opening a PR uses `.github/pull_request_template.md`; fill every section, not just the title.
108	- "Why" must answer both the author's use case (what made you write this PR) and the pain being addressed (user problem, technical debt, prod issue, or unblocker), not just a one-line restatement of the title.
109	- "What users will see" describes the change from a user's perspective — what they click, what new thing appears, what default behavior changed — not from a code perspective.
110	- The Surface area checklist must reflect actual surfaces touched; check every box that applies, including extension points (`skills/`, `design-systems/`, `design-templates/`, `craft/`), CLI flags, env vars, i18n keys, and new root `package.json` dependencies.
111	- If any UI surface is checked, attach screenshots showing the entry point — where users discover the change — not just the feature in isolation; before/after is best for behavior changes.
112	- For bug-fix PRs, link the red-spec test that reproduces the bug and confirm it went red on `main` and green on the branch, per the `Bug follow-up workflow` section above.
113	- `CONTRIBUTING.md` covers PR scope, title format, dependency policy, and the issue-first rule for non-trivial features; `docs/code-review-guidelines.md` is the reviewer-facing complement.
114	
115	## Code review guide
116	
117	- Use `docs/code-review-guidelines.md` as the repository-wide review standard. That document is the operational guide; this `AGENTS.md` is the source of truth when the two disagree.
118	- Walk reviews top-down through `docs/code-review-guidelines.md`: Product relevance test → forbidden surfaces → ownership/scope → matching lane → checklist → comments → approval bar.
119	- Pick the matching review lane: default code/tests, contract and protocol changes, design-system additions, skill additions, or craft additions.
120	- Before reviewing changes under `apps/`, `packages/`, `tools/`, or `e2e/`, read that directory's `AGENTS.md` and apply its local boundaries.
121	- Blocking review feedback should focus on correctness, security/secrets, data integrity, repository boundary violations, contract/migration breakage, missing required validation, or high-risk maintainability issues.
122	- Only maintainers may close a PR instead of requesting changes, and only when the change is not salvageable on the existing branch (wrong target product, foreign test harness, DOM/API assumptions absent from this repo, or scripts that conflict with lifecycle rules).
123	
124	## PR-duty tooling
125	
126	`pnpm tools-pr` is the maintainer-only control plane for PR-duty work on this repo. It is a thin `gh` wrapper that encodes repo-specific knowledge — review-lane derivation, forbidden-surface flags, per-lane checklists, validation-command suggestions, and a fixed dictionary of factual classify tags (`bot-only-approval`, `needs-rebase`, `stale-approval`, `unresolved-changes-requested`, `awaiting-*` timing, `org-member`, etc.). The tool is read-only on the PR surface: it never approves, merges, comments, or closes; those side effects stay in explicit `gh` invocations the maintainer runs.
127	
128	Common subcommands:
129	
130	- `pnpm tools-pr list` — triage the open queue by lane and review-state bucket.
131	- `pnpm tools-pr view <num>` — factual review brief for a single PR.
132	- `pnpm tools-pr classify --all` — script-level tag JSON for the whole open queue (entry point for cron / digest consumers); per-PR `classify <num>` for spot checks.
133	- `pnpm tools-pr assignment` — assigner-perspective ownership + idle-time / blocker view across the queue.
134	
135	For the full tag dictionary, operational playbook (direct merge / duplicate-title / awaiting-author / org-member / agent-review flows), comment templates, language-detection rules, and tool-design constraints (precision boundaries, factual-output rule, retry + pagination strategy), see [`tools/pr/AGENTS.md`](tools/pr/AGENTS.md).
136	
137	## Agent runtime conventions
138	
139	- `RuntimeAgentDef.promptInputFormat` selects how the daemon writes the prompt to a child's stdin. The default `'text'` writes the composed prompt and ends stdin immediately. `'stream-json'` wraps the prompt as one JSONL `user` message and KEEPS stdin open so the daemon can stream further user messages back in mid-turn. Claude (`apps/daemon/src/runtimes/defs/claude.ts`) ships `'stream-json'` together with `--input-format stream-json` so the host can answer interactive tools like `AskUserQuestion` with a real `tool_result` block. Every other agent stays on `'text'`.
140	- `apps/daemon/src/server.ts` tracks `run.pendingHostAnswers` (a Set of `tool_use_id` strings) and `run.stdinOpen` on the run object. The `claude-stream-json` event handler adds AskUserQuestion ids to the set and closes stdin only when both the set is empty AND a `turn_end` (or `usage`) event arrives with a non `tool_use` `stop_reason`. The `tool_use` stop reason means the model paused mid tool (waiting on claude-code's internal runner or on a host answer); closing stdin there would truncate the follow up response.
141	- `claude-stream.ts` emits the `turn_end` event AFTER iterating the assistant message's content blocks, not before. When `--include-partial-messages` is unsupported, tool_use events surface only from the assistant wrapper, so emitting `turn_end` first would let the daemon close stdin before the host had registered any pending answers.
142	- `POST /api/runs/:id/tool-result` is the daemon endpoint for feeding a `tool_result` block back into a still running stream-json child. Body shape: `{ toolUseId: string, content: string, isError?: boolean }`. Web callers use `submitChatRunToolResult` from `apps/web/src/providers/daemon.ts`. The daemon writes a JSONL `user` message containing one `tool_result` content block, removes the id from `pendingHostAnswers`, and lets the next `turn_end` decide when to close stdin.
143	- AskUserQuestion specifically: Claude's system prompt section in `apps/daemon/src/prompts/system.ts` (Claude only block at the bottom of `composeSystemPrompt`) tells the model to use the tool for 2 to 4 finite choices, and to stop generating tokens after the tool call instead of also writing a markdown duplicate. `AssistantMessage.suppressAskUserQuestionFallbackText` is the belt and suspenders that hides any trailing markdown text in the same turn.
144	
145	## Chat UI conventions
146	
147	- `apps/web/src/components/file-viewer-render-mode.ts` decides URL-load vs srcDoc for HTML previews. Bridges (deck, comment/inspect selection, palette, edit, tweaks) can ONLY inject through the srcDoc path. Add a new disqualifier to `UrlLoadDecision` whenever a feature needs a srcDoc-only bridge; pass it from `FileViewer.tsx` based on a source-content heuristic where appropriate (e.g. `hasTweaksTemplate`). The host keeps both iframes mounted simultaneously and swaps CSS visibility so toggling render mode does not cause an iframe reload flash; `iframeRef.current` stays aligned with the active iframe via `useEffect`. Receive filters use `isOurIframe(ev.source)` to accept messages from either iframe but signals that should ONLY come from the active iframe (e.g. `od:tweaks-available`) re-check `ev.source === iframeRef.current?.contentWindow`.
148	- TodoWrite UI pins one canonical task list above the chat composer via `PinnedTodoSlot` in `ChatPane.tsx`. The slot reads the latest TodoWrite snapshot across the conversation through `latestTodoWriteInputFromMessages` (`apps/web/src/runtime/todos.ts`). `AssistantMessage.stripTodoToolGroups` removes any TodoWrite tool groups from per message rendering so there is exactly one TodoCard on screen. The progress count includes both `completed` and `in_progress` items (1/4 reads "one underway" not "zero finished"). Dismissal via the Done button is keyed on the snapshot's JSON, so a fresh TodoWrite from the agent automatically re shows the card. `PinnedTodoSlot` sits OUTSIDE the `.chat-log` scroll container, so auto-scroll requires explicit coverage: `ChatPane`'s `ResizeObserver` accepts a `containerRef` from `PinnedTodoSlot` and observes that element directly, and a pane-level `MutationObserver` (`childList: true` on the chat pane ancestor) re-syncs that observation whenever the slot mounts or unmounts as new TodoWrite snapshots arrive.
149	- `AskUserQuestionCard` (in `ToolCard.tsx`) prefers the live `onAnswerToolUse(toolUseId, content)` route (POSTs to `/api/runs/:id/tool-result`) and falls back to the legacy `onSubmitForm(text)` path when the run has already terminated. Selected chips persist across reloads by parsing the stored `tool_result.content` back into the selections shape.
150	- Tool group rendering uses `dedupeSnapshotToolRetries` to collapse identical `AskUserQuestion` retries (one card per unique input, keeping the latest tool_use_id) and `TodoWrite` snapshots (only the most recent call, since each call is a state replace).
151	
152	## i18n keys
153	
154	- `apps/web/src/i18n/types.ts` is the typed `Dict`; every key must be defined in all 18 locale files under `apps/web/src/i18n/locales/*.ts` (`ar`, `de`, `en`, `es-ES`, `fa`, `fr`, `hu`, `id`, `ja`, `ko`, `pl`, `pt-BR`, `ru`, `th`, `tr`, `uk`, `zh-CN`, `zh-TW`). Add the key to `types.ts` first; missing translations produce a typecheck error.
155	
156	## UI animation philosophy
157	
158	- Default ease-out for UI transitions: `cubic-bezier(0.23, 1, 0.32, 1)`. Built-in `ease` is too weak; `ease-in` is forbidden for UI elements because it feels sluggish.
159	- Asymmetric durations: enter around 200ms, exit around 140ms. Exit reads as decisive because the user has already chosen to dismiss.
160	- Accordion expand and collapse uses `grid-template-rows: 0fr -> 1fr` (modern auto height pattern). Pair with opacity fade and the easing above. The shared `.accordion-collapsible` + `.accordion-collapsible-inner` class pair (defined in `apps/web/src/index.css`) is the canonical implementation; reuse it for new disclosure UI.
161	- Never animate from `transform: scale(0)`. Start from `scale(0.9)` or higher with `opacity: 0`.
162	- For elements that show conditionally, keep them mounted and toggle a CSS class (e.g. `.chat-jump-btn-active`). React unmounts skip the exit transition entirely.
163	
164	## Validation strategy
165	
166	- After package, workspace, or command-entry changes, run `pnpm install` so workspace links and generated dist entries stay fresh.
167	- Treat every `pnpm-lock.yaml` change as requiring a Nix pnpm deps hash refresh check. Use `pnpm nix:update-hash` to regenerate `nix/pnpm-deps.nix`, then re-run `nix flake check --print-build-logs --keep-going` (or rely on the PR `Validate workspace` gate if Nix is unavailable locally).
168	- Before marking regular work ready, run at least `pnpm guard` and `pnpm typecheck`, plus the package-scoped tests/builds that match the files changed. Do not use or add root `pnpm test`/`pnpm build` aliases.
169	- For local web runtime loops, prefer `pnpm tools-dev run web --daemon-port <port> --web-port <port>`.
170	- On a GUI-capable machine, validate desktop by running `pnpm tools-dev`, then `pnpm tools-dev inspect desktop status`.
171	- Stamp/namespace changes must validate two concurrent namespaces and run desktop `inspect eval` plus `inspect screenshot` for each namespace.
172	- Path/log changes must run `pnpm tools-dev logs --namespace <name> --json` and confirm log paths are under `.tmp/tools-dev/<namespace>/...`.
173	
174	## Bug follow-up workflow
175	
176	The following is a working playbook for routine bug follow-ups, distilled from recent practice. Treat it as a default action shape, not a contract — production reality always has edges these bullets can't anticipate, so use judgment when the situation doesn't fit cleanly.
177	
178	- **Lead with a red spec.** Default to encoding the bug as a falsifiable test that goes red before any source change, so the fix is anchored in observable behavior rather than source-code intuition. If a red spec can't be written cheaply, that's usually a signal to clarify scope rather than push forward on a guess.
179	- **Try the cheapest layer first.** Reach for the lightest test layer that can still see the symptom (e2e Vitest at the daemon HTTP boundary → app-local Vitest → Playwright UI → platform-native harnesses), and drop down only when the cheaper layer can't.
180	- **Hold the spec's scope.** Defects discovered outside the bug's described boundary belong in a follow-up — their own red spec, their own PR — not in this fix. List them in the PR body's "Adjacent issues" section with the rationale and move on.
181	- **Let the fix read as an invariant.** Prefer a named helper whose docblock describes what must hold over a bolt-on `if` guard with apologetic history-comments. The call site should read as intent.
182	- **Diff against the baseline.** When neighboring suites have pre-existing failures, stash or check out upstream before claiming "no new failures."
183	- **Link the issue from the PR body.** Use `Fixes #N` / `Closes #N` / `Resolves #N` so the issue auto-closes on merge and the release-time reverse lookup (`gh issue view N --json closedByPullRequestsReferences` → `git tag --contains <merge sha>`) actually has a chain to follow. The repo's PR template prompts for this; deleting the prompt is fine when the PR genuinely closes nothing.
184	- **Stage human verification for visible bugs.** When the symptom needs an eye to confirm — UI, platform-native behavior, animations, race conditions a unit test can't see — green specs alone aren't acceptance. Stand up a buggy-vs-fix comparison the reviewer can drive themselves (typical shape: two namespaced runtimes, one on `main`, one on the fix branch), and seed any required data only through production HTTP APIs; source-level test backdoors invalidate the verification because they prove a fake flow rather than the real one.
185	
186	For a worked example of one full loop (red e2e spec → fix → green), see `e2e/tests/dialog/stop-reconciles-message.test.ts` (issue #135).
187	
188	# Common commands
189	
190	```bash
191	pnpm install
192	pnpm nix:update-hash
193	pnpm tools-dev
194	pnpm tools-serve start updater
195	pnpm tools-dev start web
196	pnpm tools-dev run web --daemon-port 17456 --web-port 17573
197	pnpm tools-dev status --json
198	pnpm tools-dev logs --json
199	pnpm tools-dev inspect desktop status --json
200	pnpm tools-dev inspect desktop screenshot --path /tmp/open-design.png

1	# Docker deployment
2	
3	This deployment ships Open Design as a single Alpine-based runtime image. The
4	daemon serves both the API and the built Next.js static export, so there is no
5	separate nginx container.
6	
7	## Local compose
8	
9	```bash
10	cd deploy
11	OPEN_DESIGN_IMAGE=docker.io/vanjayak/open-design:latest docker compose pull
12	OPEN_DESIGN_IMAGE=docker.io/vanjayak/open-design:latest docker compose up -d --no-build
13	```
14	
15	Defaults:
16	
17	- Host port: `127.0.0.1:7456` (`OPEN_DESIGN_PORT=8080` to publish on `127.0.0.1:8080`)
18	- Runtime data volume: `open_design_data` mounted at `/app/.od`
19	- Node heap cap: `--max-old-space-size=192`
20	- Compose memory cap: `384m` (`OPEN_DESIGN_MEM_LIMIT=256m` to override)
21	
22	Do not publish the daemon directly on a public or shared LAN interface. The API is
23	unauthenticated for non-browser clients, so remote deployments should keep Compose
24	bound to localhost and put an authenticated reverse proxy, SSH tunnel, or VPN in
25	front of it.
26	
27	When exposing the service through an authenticated public IP, domain, or reverse
28	proxy, set `OPEN_DESIGN_ALLOWED_ORIGINS` to the browser origins that should be
29	allowed to call `/api`:
30	
31	```bash
32	OPEN_DESIGN_ALLOWED_ORIGINS=https://od.example.com,http://203.0.113.10:7456 docker compose up -d --no-build
33	```
34	
35	Pin a specific published image with a digest instead of the mutable `latest` tag:
36	
37	```bash
38	OPEN_DESIGN_IMAGE=docker.io/vanjayak/open-design@sha256:<digest> docker compose up -d --no-build
39	```
40	The image intentionally does not bundle Claude/Codex/Gemini CLI binaries. Keep
41	those outside the image, or build a separate private runtime layer if a server
42	deployment needs local code-agent CLIs installed in the container.
43	
44	## Publish to Docker Hub
45	
46	```bash
47	deploy/scripts/publish-images.sh --image_tag latest
48	```
49	
50	Useful overrides:
51	
52	```bash
53	IMAGE_NAMESPACE=your-dockerhub-user deploy/scripts/publish-images.sh --arch arm64
54	deploy/scripts/publish-images.sh --image docker.io/your-user/open-design:0.1.0
55	```
56	
57	The script defaults to:
58	
59	- `docker.io/vanjayak/open-design:<tag>`
60	- `linux/amd64,linux/arm64`
61	- `skopeo` push strategy with Docker credentials read from `~/.docker/config.json`
62	- preloading base images through `skopeo` to reduce Docker Hub pull flakiness
63	
64	If `127.0.0.1:7890` is available and no proxy is already set, the script uses it
65	for registry access and passes `host.docker.internal:7890` into Docker builds. The
66	host-gateway alias is only added for builds that need this local proxy mapping.
67	
68	### Colima swap helper for Apple Silicon
69	
70	`deploy/scripts/prepare-colima-build-swap.sh` is for manual Docker image
71	publishing from an Apple Silicon macOS host that uses Colima as the Docker VM.
72	The helper is intentionally Apple Silicon-only because the failure mode it covers
73	is local arm64 Colima builds exhausting a small Linux VM while preparing
74	multi-arch images. It exits before touching Colima on non-macOS or
75	non-Apple-Silicon hosts.
76	
77	Low-memory Colima VMs can run out of RAM during multi-arch image builds. The
78	helper checks the VM memory and swap status, then creates and enables a temporary
79	swap file only when the VM has no swap and less than 4 GiB of RAM. The 4 GiB
80	threshold is a conservative default for short-lived manual publishes on small
81	Colima profiles; raise `COLIMA_BUILD_SWAP_MEMORY_THRESHOLD_KIB` if larger builds
82	still OOM, or lower it if you only want swap for very small VMs.
83	
84	Prefer increasing the Colima VM memory (`colima start --memory <GiB>` or the
85	profile config) when you want a persistent build machine. Use this helper when
86	you need a temporary, reversible boost for one manual publish without resizing
87	or recreating the VM.
88	
89	Run it before a manual publish if Docker builds fail with out-of-memory errors,
90	or if `status` shows a small Colima VM with no swap. The swap remains active
91	until cleanup or VM restart, so use a shell trap for one-off sessions:
92	
93	```bash
94	deploy/scripts/prepare-colima-build-swap.sh status
95	deploy/scripts/prepare-colima-build-swap.sh
96	trap 'deploy/scripts/prepare-colima-build-swap.sh cleanup' EXIT
97	deploy/scripts/publish-images.sh --image_tag latest
98	```
99	
100	Useful overrides:
101	
102	```bash
103	COLIMA_BUILD_SWAP_SIZE=6G deploy/scripts/prepare-colima-build-swap.sh
104	COLIMA_BUILD_SWAP_MEMORY_THRESHOLD_KIB=6291456 deploy/scripts/prepare-colima-build-swap.sh
105	COLIMA_BIN=/opt/homebrew/bin/colima deploy/scripts/prepare-colima-build-swap.sh status
106	COLIMA_BUILD_SWAP_CLEANUP_FORCE=1 COLIMA_BUILD_SWAPFILE=/custom-swapfile deploy/scripts/prepare-colima-build-swap.sh cleanup
107	```
108	
109	`cleanup` removes the default helper path and the old helper path. If you set a
110	custom `COLIMA_BUILD_SWAPFILE`, cleanup refuses to remove it unless
111	`COLIMA_BUILD_SWAP_CLEANUP_FORCE=1` is also set.
112

1	# Craft references
2	
3	Brand-agnostic craft knowledge. Each file is a small, dense rulebook on one
4	dimension of professional UI craft (typography, color, motion, …). Skills
5	opt into the references they need; the daemon injects only the requested
6	ones into the system prompt above the active skill body.
7	
8	## Why a third axis next to `skills/` and `design-systems/`
9	
10	| Axis | Scope | Example |
11	|---|---|---|
12	| `skills/` | Artifact shape | `saas-landing`, `dashboard`, `pricing-page` |
13	| `design-systems/` | Brand visual language (the 9-section `DESIGN.md`) | `linear-app`, `apple`, `notion` |
14	| `craft/` | **Universal** craft knowledge — true regardless of brand | letter-spacing rules, accent-overuse caps, anti-AI-slop |
15	
16	`DESIGN.md` tells the agent which colors and fonts a brand uses. `craft/`
17	tells the agent the universal rules a competent designer applies on top —
18	e.g. ALL CAPS always needs ≥0.06em tracking, regardless of the brand.
19	
20	## How a skill opts in
21	
22	Add an `od.craft.requires` array to the skill's front-matter. Only the
23	listed sections are injected, so a skill that needs only typography pays
24	no token cost for color/motion content.
25	
26	```yaml
27	od:
28	  craft:
29	    requires: [typography, color, anti-ai-slop]
30	```
31	
32	Use the layered stack for editorial skills that require authored hierarchy
33	and sustained reading behavior:
34	
35	```yaml
36	od:
37	  craft:
38	    requires: [typography, typography-hierarchy, typography-hierarchy-editorial]
39	```
40	
41	Allowed values match the file names in this directory minus the `.md`
42	extension. Unknown values are silently ignored (forward-compatible).
43	
44	### Why silent fallback instead of fail-fast?
45	
46	A skeptical reader will ask: "If a skill requests a planned-but-not-yet-vendored
47	section and the corresponding file doesn't exist yet, shouldn't we warn
48	the user?" We chose forward-compatibility over fail-fast: a skill
49	authored today can list a planned slug and start benefiting the moment
50	the matching `craft/<slug>.md` is vendored in a follow-up PR, with no
51	skill edit needed. The cost of a missed reference is a missing
52	paragraph in the system prompt, not a broken skill — so the loud
53	failure mode is not worth the friction.
54	
55	Note for skill authors arriving from older guidance: an earlier draft
56	used `motion` as the future-slug placeholder. The shipped equivalent
57	today is `animation-discipline`. Use that one if your skill emits
58	motion.
59	
60	### Enforcement levels
61	
62	Craft files mix auto-checked rules and guidance.
63	
64	- **Auto-checked.** Rules wired into `apps/daemon/src/lint-artifact.ts` — currently the P0 list in `anti-ai-slop.md` (Tailwind-indigo accent, two-stop hero gradients, emoji-as-icons, etc.). The linter reports these as findings back to the UI (for P0/P1 badges) and to the agent (as a system reminder for self-correction). Artifact persistence is not currently hard-blocked on P0 hits.
65	- **Guidance.** The rest. The agent reads the rules, reviewers apply them, the linter doesn't check them.
66	
67	A purely behavioral craft file (state-coverage, animation-discipline) is guidance unless a specific rule is later promoted into `lint-artifact.ts`.
68	
69	## Files
70	
71	| File | Section name | When to require |
72	|---|---|---|
73	| `typography.md` | `typography` | Any skill that emits typed content (~all skills) |
74	| `typography-hierarchy.md` | `typography-hierarchy` | Any skill that emits typed content where hierarchy must feel authored, not assembled — especially surfaces with a strong entry point, varied levels, or intentional rhythm. Compose with `typography`. |
75	| `typography-hierarchy-editorial.md` | `typography-hierarchy-editorial` | Skills whose primary artifact is a sustained reading surface: `blog-post`, `docs-page`, `digital-eguide`. Requires `typography` + `typography-hierarchy`. |
76	| `color.md` | `color` | Any skill that emits styled output (~all skills) |
77	| `anti-ai-slop.md` | `anti-ai-slop` | Marketing pages, landing pages, decks |
78	| `state-coverage.md` | `state-coverage` | Any skill with stateful UI (dashboards, mobile apps, forms, list/table views) |
79	| `animation-discipline.md` | `animation-discipline` | Any skill that ships motion: mobile apps, multi-screen flows, gamified UI, transitions, microinteractions |
80	| `accessibility-baseline.md` | `accessibility-baseline` | Any skill that ships interactive UI: dashboards, forms, mobile flows, anything with focus/labels/keyboard paths |
81	| `rtl-and-bidi.md` | `rtl-and-bidi` | Any skill that ships localized text or layout: blogs, docs, financial tables, mobile apps, anything that may render Arabic / Hebrew / Persian |
82	| `form-validation.md` | `form-validation` | Any skill whose primary artifact contains an interactive form: lead capture, sign-in, signup, settings, multi-step intake |
83	| `laws-of-ux.md` | `laws-of-ux` | Any skill whose composition decisions hit named cognitive limits: pricing pages (Hick's, Choice Overload, Von Restorff), dashboards (Pareto, Selective Attention, Working Memory), onboarding (Goal-Gradient, Zeigarnik, Peak-End), modals (Fitts's, Tesler's). Sibling axis to the rendering-rule files above — covers what to compose, not how to render. |
84	
85	**Partial-stateful skills.** A skill that's mostly static but contains an embedded form, data table, or query surface should opt in. State-coverage rules apply to the stateful component, not the whole page.
86	
87	More sections (`icons`, `craft-details`) will be added in follow-up
88	PRs as we wire the linter side.
89	
90	## Attribution
91	
92	Craft content is adapted from the MIT-licensed
93	[refero_skill](https://github.com/referodesign/refero_skill) project
94	(© Refero Design), with edits to fit Open Design's house style and link
95	back to OD's design tokens (`var(--accent)` etc.) instead of generic
96	Tailwind hex values.
97
